schools.summary: Judaizing teachers came to Antioch teaching that circumcision and the law of Moses were necessary for salvation — 'except ye be circumcised... ye cannot be saved,' striking at the heart of the gospel of grace; the first great doctrinal crisis of the church.
Calvin: if salvation is tied to the law, it is no longer grounded in the grace of Christ — the error, seemingly tolerable, overthrew the free righteousness of faith and the liberty of the gospel.

debates.question: Is circumcision/keeping the law necessary for salvation?

sides.position: No — the Judaizers' demand strikes at the gospel of grace (the question the Council would settle)
Yes — 'except ye be circumcised ye cannot be saved' (the Judaizers, rejected)

themes: except ye be circumcised, ye cannot be saved

schools.summary: Paul and Barnabas, after earnest controversy (warm but not improper — it is right to contend for the faith, Barnes, Jude 3), sent to Jerusalem to refer the question; the journey identified with Galatians 2:1 (Titus the test case); the reference to the apostles and elders, not Peter alone, telling against the Roman theory (Ellicott).

Calvin: the lawful use of councils — to decide controversies by the authority of God's word, not their own pleasure; Paul not depending on the verdict (Galatians 1:8) but seeking concurrence for the church's peace.

themes: go up to Jerusalem unto the apostles and elders

schools.summary: Paul and Barnabas, sent by the church, declared the conversion of the Gentiles through Phenice and Samaria, causing great joy; the route itself asserting the principle they contended for (Ellicott); the joy contrasting with Pharisaic narrowness, a test of true Christian feeling (Barnes).

themes: declaring the conversion of the Gentiles
the journey a witness to grace

schools.summary: Paul and Barnabas received by the church, apostles, and elders (the three bodies — assembly, senate, council, Ellicott) at a public welcome (Robertson); they declared all that God had done, a preliminary meeting (Galatians 2:2).
Calvin: the report ascribed all to God — not a fellow-laborer but the whole praise his; the conversion of the Gentiles God's doing, not theirs.

themes: received of the church, apostles, and elders
declared all things that God had done

schools.summary: Pharisee believers demanded the Gentiles be circumcised and keep the law — genuine believers yet still of the Pharisee party, retaining the old narrowness (Robertson); their position Christianity as a restored Judaism, Titus's case the crucial test (Ellicott, Galatians 2).
Calvin: relics of their former nature — the old Pharisaic pride and addiction to external rites surviving their conversion.

themes: certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed
needful to circumcise and keep the law
the persistence of the law-bound mindset
the formal terms of the debate

schools.summary: The apostles and elders came together to consider the great question — the formal convening of the first ecclesiastical council (Henry); the deliberation careful, with full debate (v.7) before decision; the model of a lawful synod deciding by the word of God.

themes: the apostles and elders came together

schools.summary: After much debate (earnest inquiry, the Judaizers fully heard, Barnes, Robertson), Peter rose (his last appearance in Acts) and appealed to the conversion of Cornelius: God chose that the Gentiles should hear the gospel by his mouth — grounding the decision on God's manifest act (Acts 10).
Calvin: Peter's argument — first, that the Gentiles must not be forced to keep the law (by God's authority); second, that salvation is overthrown if the conscience is caught in that snare.

themes: the Gentiles by my mouth should hear the gospel

schools.summary: God, who knows the hearts (the characteristic Petrine epithet, Robertson), bore witness to the Gentiles by giving them the Spirit, even as to the Jews — the decisive evidence (at Caesarea, Acts 10:44-46, even before baptism, Robertson, Clarke), making no distinction.

themes: God, which knoweth the hearts, bare them witness
giving them the Holy Ghost, even as us
the gift of the Spirit the decisive evidence

schools.summary: God put no difference between Jew and Gentile, purifying their hearts by faith — treating both alike (Robertson), the wall of separation broken (Ephesians 2:14); acceptance not by rites but by faith alone (Barnes), a thoroughly Pauline note from Peter (Robertson).
Calvin: justification by faith — the heart purified not by works but by faith receiving the cleanness offered in Christ, not a quality poured into the soul.

themes: put no difference between us and them

schools.summary: Peter's challenge — 'why tempt ye God?' to impose a yoke neither the fathers nor they could bear; to tempt God was to resist his manifest will (Robertson, Ellicott); the yoke the burdensome ceremonial law (Barnes), an unbearable burden.

Calvin presses to the moral impossibility — the law, exacting perfect obedience under a curse, cannot be kept by fallen man (against the Pelagian/Jerome reading of keepability); to impose it on the Gentiles is to tempt God and drown souls in despair.

debates.question: Is the 'yoke' the ceremonial law only, or the law in its moral impossibility?

sides.position: The law as exacting perfect obedience under a curse, impossible for fallen man (against Pelagius/Jerome)

themes: a yoke neither our fathers nor we were able to bear
the impossibility of keeping the law

schools.summary: Peter's climax — 'through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved, even as they,' a thoroughly Pauline doctrine of grace (Robertson) placing Jew and Gentile on the same level, both saved by grace not the law (Barnes); Peter's last word in Acts and his clearest.
Calvin: grace and the law opposed — those justified by faith are free from the yoke of the law, seeking salvation in Christ's grace alone.

themes: through the grace of the Lord Jesus we shall be saved
salvation by grace, Jew and Gentile alike

schools.summary: After Peter's speech the multitude fell silent and gave audience to Barnabas and Paul, who declared the miracles God wrought among the Gentiles — the signs God's seal of approval (Ellicott), confirming Peter's doctrine with the facts; Barnabas named first (better known in Jerusalem, Robertson).
Calvin: the silence a sign the Spirit reigned in the assembly, all yielding to reason once Peter laid open the counsel of God.

themes: what miracles God had wrought among the Gentiles
the harmony of doctrine and fact

schools.summary: James, as president, spoke last and summed up the matter (Chrysostom); the witnesses draw the anti-papal conclusion — that James, not Peter, gave the decisive judgment refutes the Roman claim of Peter's primacy (Barnes, Clarke); James the Lord's brother, called an apostle (Galatians 1:19).
Calvin: the impudency of the Pope refuted — the decree set down at James's appointment rather than Peter's; even Eusebius calls James 'the Bishop of the Apostles.'

debates.question: Does the Council support Peter's papal primacy?

sides.position: No — James, not Peter, presided and gave the decisive judgment, refuting the Roman claim

themes: Men and brethren, hearken unto me

schools.summary: James confirms Peter's testimony ('Simeon,' the Aramaic form, Robertson) — God visited the Gentiles to take from them 'a people for his name'; the Gentiles, formerly the 'nations,' now as truly God's people as Israel (Ellicott), a chosen people out of the Gentiles.

James appeals to 'the words of the prophets' (Amos 9:11-12 as representative) to confirm the Gentile calling — testing the fact by Scripture (Barnes); the principle that even the most striking work of God must accord with the Bible.
Calvin: the apostles' submission to Scripture — proving their doctrine from the word, not taking authority to themselves; the prophets cited (plural) though one is quoted, for all agree.

themes: to this agree the words of the prophets
the church's submission to the word

schools.summary: James cites Amos 9:11 — the fallen tabernacle of David rebuilt — figuring the throne/kingdom of David (Robertson), now rebuilt by Christ the Son of David (Ellicott); the Jewish tradition understood it of the Messiah (Lightfoot, Clarke).
Calvin: the rebuilding of David's fallen house is the establishment of Christ's kingdom, which includes the Gentiles — the prophecy properly pertaining to Christ.

themes: I will build again the tabernacle of David
the kingdom of Christ the Son of David
the prophetic foundation of the Gentile calling

schools.summary: The heart of Amos — 'the residue of men and all the Gentiles' seeking the Lord — a clear prediction of the Gentiles' inclusion without Jewish rites (Barnes); the argument turning on the LXX reading (vs the Hebrew 'possess the remnant of Edom'), proving the discussion was in Greek (Ellicott).

themes: the residue of men might seek after the Lord
all the Gentiles upon whom my name is called
the LXX reading proving Greek was used

schools.summary: 'Known unto God are all his works from the beginning' — the answer to the charge of innovation; the Gentile calling God's eternal purpose, no novelty (Ellicott), foretold and planned, not to be resisted (Barnes).

Wesley: an unanswerable proof that God foreknows future contingencies — his eternal knowledge of his works including the free acts of men; we ought not to think the Gentile calling a new or strange thing.

themes: known unto God are all his works
from the beginning of the world
the Gentile calling God's eternal purpose

schools.summary: James's judgment — 'trouble not them which from among the Gentiles are turned to God' — relieving the Gentiles from the burden of the law (Barnes, Ellicott); authoritative but not dictatorial, summing up as president (Robertson).
Calvin: the four abstentions (v.20) do not burden the conscience but serve brotherly concord — the Gentiles free before God, the precepts a matter of peace not law.

themes: trouble not them which are turned to God

schools.summary: Four abstentions — pollutions of idols, fornication, things strangled, blood — a compromise drawn from the Noachic precepts to avoid offense to the Jews (Ellicott); fornication included because it was the universal pagan vice, not held disgraceful, often tied to idolatry (Barnes); blood resting on Genesis 9:4.
Calvin: the abstentions temporary, for concord, not perpetual law; fornication especially concubinage, common among the Gentiles; the precepts serving peace not the conscience.

debates.question: Are the four abstentions permanent law or a temporary expedient for concord?

sides.position: A temporary expedient for concord/peace with the Jews, not perpetual law

themes: abstain from pollutions of idols and fornication

schools.summary: The common reading: the Jews, hearing Moses read every sabbath, revere the law and would be offended if the Gentiles disregarded these things; the abstentions preserve peace, the Jews needing no instruction (Barnes, Ellicott).

Calvin's alternative: the ceremonies cannot be abolished all at once, the Jews being long accustomed; concord must be kept for a season until the liberty of Christ appears more plainly — the old ceremonies buried with honour.

debates.question: What is the sense of 'Moses hath in every city them that preach him'?

sides.position: The Jews need no instruction (hearing Moses weekly); the abstentions are for the Gentiles to avoid offending them
The ceremonies must be phased out gradually, concord kept for a season

themes: Moses hath in every city them that preach him
read in the synagogues every sabbath

schools.summary: The apostles, elders, and whole church unanimously decided to send chosen delegates — Judas Barsabas and Silas, chief men — to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas; the delegates sent to authenticate the decree, that there be no suspicion of Paul and Barnabas alone (Chrysostom); the whole church concurring (Henry).

themes: the apostles and elders, with the whole church
Judas Barsabas and Silas, chief men

schools.summary: The apostles, elders, and brethren wrote to the Gentile brethren of Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia — the standard epistolary greeting from the whole church; the warmth of the address (calling them 'brethren,' Chrysostom) recognizing the Gentiles as full members of the church.

themes: the apostles and elders and brethren send greeting
unto the brethren which are of the Gentiles

schools.summary: The letter disavows the Judaizers — 'certain which went out from us have troubled you... to whom we gave no such commandment'; they had troubled and subverted the souls of the Gentiles, acting without apostolic authority (Chrysostom); the strong language marking the gravity of their soul-subverting error.

themes: certain which went out from us have troubled you
to whom we gave no such commandment

schools.summary: The Council, 'assembled with one accord,' decided to send delegates with 'our beloved Barnabas and Paul' — the unanimity of the decision and the warm commendation of the apostles, honoring them against the Judaizers' disparagement (Chrysostom); Barnabas named first (better known in Jerusalem).

The apostles honor Paul and Barnabas as 'men that have hazarded their lives for the name of Christ' — a high tribute referring to their sufferings (Paul stoned at Lystra, 14:19); the highest commendation, set against the Judaizers' meddling, the mark of true servants of Christ.

themes: men that have hazarded their lives
for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ

schools.summary: Judas and Silas sent to confirm the letter by word — delivering the same message verbally (Robertson), authenticating the written decree; the wisdom of confirming the written word by living witnesses, that the Gentile churches might be fully assured.

themes: we have sent Judas and Silas
tell you the same things by mouth

schools.summary: 'It seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us' — a strong claim to inspiration (Barnes, Robertson); the 'necessary things' necessary for the time and for concord, not perpetual law (Ellicott, Wesley), a light burden compared to the freedom won (Robertson).

Calvin: the Spirit the guide, the apostles his ministers, adding nothing to God's word but applying charity to their time; strongly rebuts the Papist use for conciliar authority — the necessity temporary (avoiding offense), the decree later abolished by Paul (Romans 14:14; 1 Corinthians 10).

debates.question: Does 'it seemed good to the Holy Ghost and to us' support permanent conciliar authority to bind consciences?

sides.position: No — the Council added nothing to God's word; the necessity was temporary, for concord, later abolished by Paul

themes: it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us
no greater burden than these necessary things

schools.summary: The decree restates the four abstentions with the gracious close 'fare ye well' (the Greek epistolary close); the temporary vs permanent nature discussed — a concordat for the time, Paul never appealing to it (1 Corinthians 8-10), Hooker citing it as a divine command given only for a season (Ellicott).
Wesley: the gentle close a blessing not a curse, contrasting with the later councils' anathemas; the blood prohibition's long history in the Greek Church but abandonment in the West.

themes: abstain from meats offered to idols, blood, things strangled, fornication
ye shall do well, fare ye well
the gracious close vs later anathemas

schools.summary: The delegates came to Antioch and gathered the whole church to deliver the apostolic letter — returning the decision to its origin (the controversy began at Antioch, v.1); the whole church assembled to hear the decree, the orderly delivery of the Council's decision.

themes: gathered the multitude together, delivered the epistle
the decision returned to its origin

schools.summary: The Antioch church, reading the letter, rejoiced for the consolation — glad the decree freed them from the burden of the law and the trouble of the Judaizers; the 'consolation' the comfort of being assured of their freedom and acceptance by grace.
Calvin: the joy the fruit of the decision — the controversy settled, their liberty affirmed, their consciences set free from the demand of the law.

themes: which when they had read, they rejoiced
the joy at the gospel of grace

schools.summary: Judas and Silas, being prophets, exhorted and confirmed the brethren — not mere bearers of the letter but ministers of the word, using their prophetic gift for the church's edification; the pastoral fruit of the delegation.
Calvin: the value of confirmation — the brethren, having received the decree, further strengthened by the prophets' exhortation, the ministry of the word continuing to build up the church.

themes: exhorted the brethren and confirmed them

schools.summary: After a stay at Antioch, Judas and Silas dismissed 'in peace' to Jerusalem — a cordial parting; the contrast with the earlier 'dissension' (v.2) marking the resolution of the conflict, the controversy ending in peace (Chrysostom: 'No more factions and fightings').

themes: they were let go in peace
from the brethren unto the apostles

schools.summary: Silas remained at Antioch (the verse textually uncertain, absent from the best MSS, perhaps added to explain his availability for v.40, Robertson); the providential result — Silas available to become Paul's companion on the second journey, a proof of his interest in the Gentile mission (Ellicott).

themes: it pleased Silas to abide there still
Silas available for the second journey

schools.summary: Paul and Barnabas continued at Antioch, teaching and preaching with many others — a flourishing ministry after the controversy was settled (Chrysostom: 'thenceforth Paul taught'); the Antioch church prospering in the word, the interval before the second journey.

themes: Paul and Barnabas continued in Antioch
the interval before the second journey

schools.summary: Paul proposed to revisit the churches of the first journey — a pastoral concern to see how the believers fared and strengthen them (Henry); the 'see how they do' displaying his fatherly care; the proposal leading, through the dispute over Mark (vv.37-39), to two missions.

themes: let us go again and visit our brethren
the proposal for the second journey

schools.summary: Barnabas wished to take John Mark again (persistently, Robertson) — moved by natural affection for his cousin (Clarke, Ellicott, Colossians 4:10), wishing to give him another trial.
Calvin: a balanced treatment — Barnabas had a fair reason to pardon the repentant Mark, yet Mark's offense (deserting the work, 13:13) was graver than commonly thought, a forsaking of his calling.

themes: Barnabas determined to take John Mark
the tender root of the dispute

schools.summary: Paul firmly refused to take Mark, because Mark had deserted the work at Pamphylia (13:13) — deeming him unfit, unable to trust one who had flinched and quit (Barnes, Wesley); Paul's reason principled, not personal — the work too important to risk on a quitter (Robertson).

themes: Paul thought not good to take him
who departed from them from Pamphylia

schools.summary: The contention so sharp (a paroxysm, Robertson) that Paul and Barnabas parted, Barnabas taking Mark to Cyprus; the providential result — two missions and Mark's later restoration (2 Timothy 4:11); judgment goes with Paul, the heart with Barnabas (Robertson).
Clarke, Wesley: no sin or anger necessarily — the word (paroxysm) need not imply it; the apostles differed strenuously but without wrong temper, Paul maintaining the right 'with love.'

debates.question: Was the sharp contention between Paul and Barnabas sinful?

sides.position: Not necessarily — they differed strenuously but without wrong temper or anger
It involved human frailty even in holy men, overruled by providence for good

schools.summary: Paul chose Silas (a fit replacement — gifted in prophecy, a Roman citizen 16:37, prominent in Jerusalem, Ellicott, Robertson) and departed, commended by the brethren to grace; the church's sympathy with Paul (Robertson), the mission continuing despite the breach.
Wesley: how mighty the grace of God, overcoming all opposition; Paul, commended to grace, departs on the second journey despite the contention.

themes: recommended by the brethren unto the grace of God
the mission continues despite the breach

schools.summary: Paul went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches — regions where churches already existed (15:23, founded in his earlier years, Galatians 1:21, Gill); the pastoral purpose to strengthen the believers (as 14:22), carrying the apostolic decree (16:4); the second journey begun.

themes: he went through Syria and Cilicia
